items.briefing:
Australian energy provider Origin has suffered a data breach exposing client information — if you're an Origin customer, be alert for follow-up phishing emails or scam calls using your personal details. Separately, business email compromise (BEC) remains one of the most common cyber incidents, where criminals impersonate executives or suppliers to trick staff into transferring money or sharing sensitive data. A phishing campaign is also actively targeting universities, so staff and students should be especially cautious with unexpected emails containing links or attachments.

Australia's incoming anti-money laundering (AML) reforms will require organisations to have customer data well-organised and accessible, so now is a good time to review how your organisation stores and manages client records.

**Practical tip:** If you receive an unexpected email asking you to transfer funds or update payment details — even if it appears to come from a manager — always verify the request by calling the person directly using a known phone number.

featured.title: Australian energy provider Origin says data breach exposes client data - BleepingComputer
featured.quote: Major Australian energy provider breached, exposing customer data to potential misuse and fraud.
